issues.description: This duplicate detection is effectively defensive-only: the compiler's main pipeline in lib.rs:3899-3918 already filters duplicate Structure/Occurrence declarations with an `is_first_def` check before they are pushed into `templates`, and lib.rs:3607-3625 already emits a proper span-labeled `duplicate entity definition 'X'` error for the user. In a real compile, the new error cannot fire on user input — it only fires when a caller constructs `templates` directly (e.g., the unit test). That makes the new diagnostic (a) duplicative of the higher-quality upstream error if the upstream filter were ever bypassed, and (b) a signal of a compiler-invariant violation rather than a user error.
issues.suggested_fix: Either (a) demote to a `debug_assert!(!name_to_idx.contains_key(...), "duplicate template name reached SCC pass — upstream filter invariant violated")` so the bug is loud in debug and silent in release, or (b) keep the error but phrase it as an internal invariant violation (e.g. "internal compiler error: duplicate template name 'X' reached recursive-structure detection") so users don't see a redundant, less-informative error on top of the upstream one.

issues.description: The error message surfaces internal Vec indices ("indices 0 and 1") which are not user-actionable — users can't map them back to source. Elsewhere in the compiler (lib.rs:3590-3600, 3609-3622, 3629-3643), duplicate-entity errors are attached to source spans via `DiagnosticLabel`. TopologyTemplate has no `span` field, so span labels aren't available here, which is another reason this diagnostic belongs at the syntax/semantic layer (where it already exists), not in the SCC pass.
issues.suggested_fix: Drop the `indices {} and {}` detail from the message; it's confusing without context. If retained, make the message self-describing (e.g. "internal: duplicate template name 'X' in SCC pass — this indicates a bug in the upstream duplicate filter").

issues.description: The comment "silently overwriting (which would corrupt the adjacency graph)" is a bit misleading. The prior `.collect::<HashMap>()` would have kept the LAST entry, not silently corrupted anything — it would just have shadowed the first. The new code deliberately keeps the FIRST entry, which matches the upstream `is_first_def` semantics in lib.rs:3899. Future readers need to know why 'first wins' is the correct choice (consistency with which template was actually compiled).
issues.suggested_fix: Rewrite the comment to reference the upstream invariant: "Keep the first entry so this map agrees with lib.rs's `is_first_def` filter, which only compiles the first definition of a given name. If a duplicate still reaches here, the upstream filter has been bypassed — emit a diagnostic and move on."

issues.description: The new `duplicate_template_names_emits_diagnostic` test only asserts that some diagnostic containing the word 'duplicate' is produced. It does not verify the non-trivial behavior of the defensive path: (a) that 'first wins' — the kept index is the first occurrence, so references from other templates resolve to the first, not the second; (b) that the shadowed duplicate is not spuriously tagged `is_recursive`; (c) that the SCC analysis still produces sane output. These are the actual behaviors readers of the new code will want documented.
issues.suggested_fix: Extend the test to construct e.g. three templates `[A(refs=[]), A(refs=[self]), B(refs=[A])]`, call `detect_recursive_structures`, and assert: (1) a duplicate diagnostic fires; (2) templates[0].is_recursive == false (first A, no self-ref); (3) templates[1].is_recursive == false (shadowed duplicate's self-ref doesn't re-enter a cycle via resolution through name_to_idx[A]==0); (4) no spurious cycle warning is emitted. Or, if the simpler behavior is preferred, at least assert the diagnostic severity is Error and the kept template's is_recursive state matches expectation.

issues.description: The insertion pattern (`get` then `insert`) does a double HashMap lookup per entry. Rust's idiomatic `Entry::Vacant`/`Entry::Occupied` API does this in one lookup and is the pattern already used elsewhere in this codebase (see lib.rs:4063 `seen.entry(key)` in the function-signature duplicate check). Using the same idiom here improves both performance and consistency with the rest of the compiler.

comprehensive_reviewer.summary: The change correctly converts a `.collect::<HashMap>()` into an explicit loop so that duplicate template names don't silently overwrite each other in the name→index map. The logic is sound and the chosen 'first-wins' semantic matches the upstream `is_first_def` filter in lib.rs, so the defensive path cannot produce an incoherent adjacency graph. Nothing is broken. However, this safeguard is largely dead code in the real pipeline — lib.rs already filters duplicate Structure/Occurrence declarations and emits a span-labeled `duplicate entity definition` error before anything reaches `detect_recursive_structures`. The new diagnostic therefore fires only if the upstream invariant is broken (i.e., a compiler bug) and surfaces internal Vec indices that are not user-actionable. Consider demoting to a `debug_assert!` or reframing the message as an internal invariant violation, and using the `Entry` API for consistency with lib.rs:4063. The new test proves a diagnostic fires but doesn't lock in the interesting behaviors (first-wins, shadowed duplicate not mis-tagged as recursive, SCC still sane) — worth expanding. No blocking issues.
